result:
Mid-afternoon.  
Somewhere outside, gravel pops under tires and a dog barks at the echo. Inside, the apartment has gone quiet the way only half-finished chores can create: laundry sulked into a drum, dishes stacked like suspect alibis under running water no one remembered to shut off. I sit at the desk and pretend the coffee left in the mug is still warm enough to finish—consoling myself with the thin fiction that productivity can be summoned by the mere stewardship of a ceramic object. Steam has long since signed its name on the window and vanished, leaving a ghost-parchment of itself between me and the winter yard.

On the sill a yellowed almanac holds the window open like a fractured doorstop. Someone—past-me?—tried to grow basil in an up-cycled yogurt cup; only spindly threads remain, each leaf a miniature apology. I flip the almanac’s brittle pages over the basil corpse, pretending I’m consulting it for optimal planting dates instead of avoiding the email drafts that blink accusingly in the corner of my screen. Every subject line is a smoke alarm with dead batteries: chirping symmetrically enough to be ignorable, poisonous enough to color the room if you stay inside long enough.

My phone lights up. An app politely offers I could be deadlifting 200 pounds right this second, followed by a targeted ad promising artisanal beeswax salve to cure the nonexistent rash I apparently collect in the cradle between thumb and forefinger. I imagine tiny bee therapists massaging my hypothetical affliction in circles that smell of eucalyptus and abandoned hobbies.

Across the alley, Mrs. Munro draws her curtains. We have conducted an entire marriage of glances from opposing windows. Some days she waves. Some days she doesn’t. Today she merely lowers the blinds like stage curtains, and I understand I have been demoted from neighbor to scenery. I accept this with the grace of any condemned backdrop: lingering just out of focus, already half-scrutinized by tomorrow’s audience.

Somewhere in the city a piano student repeats the same four transitional bars—badly—and each time the phrase stumbles I feel wires tighten under my sternum. It’s the music of rehearsed failure, and I recognize the texture, how it feels to circle endlessly in the foyer of completion. I open the draft email titled “RE: availability for launch prep Q1” and close it again, as if the act of toggling the lid on the problem might allow some trapped oxygen to escape.

I think: maybe the difference between a sentence and a sentence fragment is only courage. One keeps a verb, the other is just the narrator’s hand frozen halfway to the audience before it decides whether to wave or shield the eyes from the spotlight.

Another car door slams. Basil sighs its last chlorophyll into the afternoon. The dog barks again, half-hearted—as if fidelity itself were embarrassed by neighborhood acoustics. I finally drop the polite sip of cold coffee into the sink. The sound itonates against porcelain like someone rapping on hollow glass, asking to be let back inside. All the unfinished syllables of the day gather behind my tongue, fattening on seconds.

I reopen the draft.  
Subject line: “RE: availability for launch prep Q1”  
Cursor blinks. Once. Twice.  
I type:

I am mostly available, except for the small square of silence I reserve at 3:17 p.m. to remember which version of me set this appointment, and how sure he was the future would still want what yesterday was willing to promise.

I delete it.

I type instead:

Yes, that slot works. Talk soon.

Send.  
The window admits a thin exhalation of winter, or maybe it’s the building itself releasing held breath. Outside, the dog finds something in the hedge to bark at (or for). Mrs. Munro’s curtains twitch: curtain callvertebrate, barely alive, maybe reconsidering scenic arrangements. The piano student finally nails the landing on the fourth try—brief major chord—then slams the fallboard like applause.

What I mean is: we keep finagling rehearsals for arrival, and the curtain keeps rising on the next run-through. I stand in my own footlights, arms half-raised like a bridge unsure whether to let traffic under or over. Somewhere in the script margins, the basil and the almanac are exchanging notes about permanence. The audience, for once, forgives us everything.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
